title: Failure Runtime Loss Guide
summary: Understand how failures, lost battery strings, failed UPS modules, and degraded capacity reduce runtime.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates how much runtime may be lost when part of a backup power system fails or becomes unavailable.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch power or runtime issues before installation.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when evaluating UPS redundancy, battery string failure, module failure, maintenance bypass conditions, or degraded backup power scenarios.
bullets: Early power design planning.
Backup runtime review.
Expansion or load-growth evaluation.
Explaining power tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions explain what each input represents in practical power and runtime planning terms.

items.label: Normal Runtime
items.whyItMatters: Normal runtime is the baseline for comparison.
items.commonMistake: Only documenting normal runtime and ignoring degraded cases.

items.label: Failed Component
items.description: The part of the system assumed unavailable.
items.whyItMatters: The failed component determines how much capacity is lost.
items.commonMistake: Assuming all failures have the same runtime impact.

items.label: Remaining Capacity
items.description: Capacity still available after the failure.
items.commonMistake: Forgetting that runtime may drop faster than capacity percentage suggests.

items.label: Critical Load
items.description: Load that still needs backup during the failure.
items.whyItMatters: Higher load reduces runtime from remaining capacity.
items.commonMistake: Leaving non-critical loads connected during degraded operation.

items.label: Reserve Requirement
items.description: Minimum runtime or capacity that must remain after failure.
items.whyItMatters: Reserve requirement defines whether degraded operation is acceptable.
items.commonMistake: Accepting degraded runtime without checking the requirement.

items.label: Failure Scenario
items.whyItMatters: Different scenarios can produce different runtime losses.
items.commonMistake: Testing only the easiest failure case.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means runtime remains acceptable after the failure. Watch means degraded runtime should be reviewed. Risk means the system may not meet runtime requirements after a failure.
bullets: Healthy: the design appears manageable based on the entered assumptions.
Watch: the design may still work, but power, runtime, or reserve assumptions deserve review.
Risk: reduce load, add capacity, improve efficiency, or validate the design more carefully before implementation.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most power planning problems come from trusting nameplate ratings, ignoring losses, or designing for normal load instead of outage behavior.
bullets: Sizing from best-case runtime.
Ignoring power factor, efficiency, battery age, or reserve margin.
Leaving non-critical loads on backup power.
Failing to document assumptions before the system is built.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer documentation, electrical code requirements, battery manufacturer guidance, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Manufacturer ratings still matter.
Battery condition and temperature can change runtime.
Real loads should be measured when possible.
A design with reserve is usually easier to support and explain.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, electrical review, manufacturer validation, code compliance review, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against actual equipment, batteries, and electrical conditions.
Confirm owner requirements and outage expectations.
Document assumptions before implementation.
